config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I do not think society should automatically come before the individual, because that can easily become an excuse to ignore basic rights and silence people who are vulnerable. In my view, a fair society protects individual rights first, then asks people to contribute to the common good within those limits. From my perspective, the best balance is not sacrificing the person for society, but building a society strong enough to respect both.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe society should automatically take priority over the individual, because in the United States that logic has too often been used to justify trampling free speech, due process, and minority rights. Society matters, but it is strongest when it is limited by individual rights rather than allowed to override them whenever the majority feels like it. I also do not buy the idea that protecting individual rights is somehow selfish; in practice, those rights are what keep society fair and prevent power from becoming abusive. The real question is not whether society or the individual comes first, but which rights are truly nonnegotiable and when, if ever, collective needs can justify narrow limits.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should automatically outrank the rights of the individual. From an Indonesian perspective, I know how easily “for the common good” can be used to pressure people to stay quiet, especially when the people in power claim they speak for society. Of course society matters a lot here too, because we value gotong royong and social harmony, but those values lose their moral force if they are used to excuse injustice or crush minority voices. The better principle is that society should pursue the common good without crossing the line of basic rights, not by treating the individual as disposable.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual, because in the United States that idea has too often been used to justify censorship, discrimination, and abuse of power. That said, I do not believe individual rights are absolute in every case; public safety, emergencies, and basic civic order can justify narrow limits when they are clearly defined and accountable. What I reject is the broad claim that society should come first, because once that becomes the default, it is too easy for majorities or governments to decide whose rights are expendable. A healthy society is not one that sacrifices the person for the collective, but one that protects individual rights precisely so the collective cannot become oppressive.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual, because in Indonesia I have seen how easily “the common good” can become a cover for silencing criticism and protecting those already in power. I do value gotong royong and social harmony, but those ideas are meaningful only if they do not erase basic rights or turn minorities into sacrifices for majority comfort. Public safety and emergencies can justify limited restrictions, but they must be narrow, transparent, and accountable, not a blank check for society to override the person. A society is stronger when it protects individuals from abuse, not when it asks them to disappear for the collective.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual, because in the United States that has too often been the language used to excuse censorship, discrimination, and government overreach. That said, I do not treat individual rights as unlimited; public safety and genuine emergencies can justify narrow, accountable restrictions. What I reject is the vague idea that "society" gets the first claim, because that usually means whoever has power gets to define the common good and decide whose rights can be ignored. In my view, a free society is one that protects the individual precisely so the collective cannot become oppressive.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
